figures.caption: FIG. 12. Log-log plots of the nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate T−1 1 and spin-spin relaxation rate T−1 2 against the reduced temperature (T − TN)/TN.

FIG. 1. Crystal structure of CuCrP2S6. (a )View along the c∗ direction showing the CuCrP2S6 layer with Cr3+ ions forming a two-dimensional triangular magnetic lattice within a network of [P2S6]4− units. (b): Local bonding environment of the P–P dimer. Each P–P dimer is coordinated to the same three Cr3+ ions within the layer. (c) & (d): Side view along the a direction illustrating the stacking of quasi-2D layers separated by van der Waals gaps. (c): High-temperature (T = 300 K) configuration in the monoclinic C2/c structure, where Cu ions occupy two (or four) partially occupied sites. (d): Low-temperature (T = 64 K) antiferroelectric structure, where Cu ions order into alternating up- and down-displacement positions, as indicated by the arrows, thereby lowering the crystal symmetry.
FIG. 11. Temperature dependence of spin-spin relaxation rate T−1 2 (T ) measured at 2.5 T applied along H ∥ c∗ .

FIG. 3. The measured 31P NMR shift Kraw, contribution from bulk magnetic effects Kd, and the intrinsic shift (K = Kraw −Kd) as a function of out-of-plane angle θ, where θ = 0◦ represents H ∥ c∗ and θ = 90◦ represents H ∥ b (b): K as a function of in-plane angle ϕ, where ϕ = 0◦ represents H ∥ a and θ = 90◦ represents H ∥ b. Solid lines represent the fit using Eq. 3 as described in the text.
